items.note: The set phrase for a first meeting. Always comes first in a self-introduction (自己紹介, jikoshoukai). 初めて (hajimete) = for the first time. The て-form here signals an ongoing state — 'this is the first time (that I am meeting you)'. Follow it with your name.

と申します (to moushimasu) is the humble/formal form of と言います (to iimasu = to say/to be called). Use this in formal or professional contexts. Casual version: ニコラといいます (Nikora to iimasu) or simply ニコラです (Nikora desu). Nicolas → ニコラ in katakana (ス is sometimes added: ニコラス).

items.script: スコットランドから来ました
items.kana: すこっとらんどからきました
items.note: から = from, 来ました = came (past tense of 来る, to come). Scotland = スコットランド. You may also hear people ask: どちらから来ましたか (dochira kara kimashita ka = where did you come from?). Saying you're from Scotland will often be met with curiosity and questions about whisky and kilts.

items.script: エジンバラに住んでいます
items.kana: えじんばらにすんでいます
items.note: に住んでいます = live in (place). 住む (sumu) = to live/reside. て-form + います describes an ongoing state. Edinburgh = エジンバラ. If you want to add the university: エジンバラ大学のそばに住んでいます (I live near the University of Edinburgh).

items.script: エジンバラ大学の研究者です
items.kana: えじんばらだいがくのけんきゅうしゃです
english: I am a researcher at the University of Edinburgh
items.note: 研究者 (kenkyuusha) = researcher (研究 = research, 者 = person). の here marks possession/affiliation: 'a researcher of the University of Edinburgh'. 大学 (daigaku) = university (大 = big, 学 = learning). If asked what you research, use item 6.

items.script: 量子コンピューティングを研究しています
items.kana: りょうしこんぴゅーてぃんぐをけんきゅうしています
romaji: ryoushi konpyuutingu wo kenkyuu shite imasu
items.note: 量子 (ryoushi) = quantum (量 = quantity/amount, 子 = small particle). コンピューティング is borrowed from English. を研究しています = 'am researching ~' (ongoing). You can simplify to 量子コンピュータの研究者です (ryoushi konpyuuta no kenkyuusha desu = I am a quantum computer researcher) if you want it shorter.

items.kana: にじゅうはっさいです
items.note: 歳 (sai) = age/years old. Numbers: 二十 (nijuu) = 20, 八 (hachi/hachi → hass before sai by sound change) = 8. Age is asked more freely in Japan than in the West — it helps establish the right level of politeness to use. Don't be surprised if it comes up early in a conversation.

items.script: 日本語を勉強しています
items.kana: にほんごをべんきょうしています
items.note: 勉強 (benkyou) = study (勉 = diligence, 強 = strong). をしています = 'am doing ~'. You can add まだ (mada = still/yet) at the start: まだ勉強しています (I'm still studying) — modest and relatable. Japanese people will be visibly pleased that you're making the effort.

items.script: 日本語が少し話せますが、あまり上手ではありません
items.kana: にほんごがすこしはなせますが、あまりじょうずではありません
romaji: nihongo ga sukoshi hanasemasu ga, amari jouzu dewa arimasen
english: I can speak a little Japanese, but I'm not very good
items.note: The classic humble opener. が here is a conjunction meaning 'but'. あまり + negative = 'not very'. 上手 (jouzu) = skilled/good at. In Japanese culture, self-deprecation is a virtue — even fluent speakers say this. It lowers expectations and makes people more likely to speak simply and clearly with you.

items.kana: りょうしりきがくがせんもんです
items.note: 専門 (senmon) = speciality/major field. 量子力学 (ryoushi rikigaku) = quantum mechanics (量子 = quantum, 力学 = mechanics/dynamics). If you prefer the computing angle: 量子情報科学 (ryoushi jouhou kagaku) = quantum information science. Japanese researchers will likely know these terms well.

items.script: 日本の文化にとても興味があります
items.kana: にほんのぶんかにとてもきょうみがあります
romaji: nihon no bunka ni totemo kyoumi ga arimasu
english: I am very interested in Japanese culture
items.note: 興味 (kyoumi) = interest, があります = I have. に marks the target of interest. とても = very. You can swap 文化 (bunka = culture) for 歴史 (rekishi = history), 食べ物 (tabemono = food), アニメ (anime), or 科学 (kagaku = science). This phrase always goes down well.

items.script: よろしくお願いします
items.kana: よろしくおねがいします
english: pleased to meet you / I look forward to working with you
items.note: The closing phrase of every self-introduction — untranslatable but essential. よろしく comes from よろしい (good/appropriate). お願いします = please (I make a request). Together: 'please regard me favourably' or 'please treat me well'. Used constantly: when exchanging business cards, starting a project, or finishing a meeting. Bow slightly when you say it.

items.kana: ごしつもんがありますか
items.note: A polite way to invite questions after your self-intro. ご is an honorific prefix. 質問 (shitsumon) = question. If you can't answer: わかりません (I don't know) or 難しい質問ですね (muzukashii shitsumon desu ne = that's a difficult question!) work well.
